data.title: Pool Corporation: Stabilizing But Not Recovering
data.description:
Shares of Pool Corporation (POOL) have been a very poor performer over the past year, losing 37% of their value. The company has struggled with a persistently weak pool construction market as consumers defer expensive discretionary purchases. With an elevated valuation, shares had little room for a prolonged slowdown, leading to significant underperformance. I last covered shares of Pool Corporation in May when I reiterated the stock as a “sell,” and since then, shares have lost 22% of their value, justifying my bearishness. With updated financials and shares approaching my $225 price target, now is a good time to revisit POOL.

Pool Corp’s business has been negatively impacted by a severe downturn in construction activity. As you can see below, its business surged post-COVID. Housing turnover was high, interest rates were low, and consumers were spending more around their houses, given remote and hybrid work. Earnings basically tripled from 2019 to 2022. Since then, we have seen earnings fall by ~40%. A sharp Fed rate hiking cycle has made borrowing to fund large projects more costly, while years of elevated inflation have sapped consumers’ savings and willingness to spend. Plus, some of the 2021-2022 construction activity likely represented a pull-forward of future demand that has since normalized.

It is important to remember that Pool’s business is not as cyclical as new pool construction. The majority of the business is tied to ongoing maintenance and repair work. While some activities can be deferred, others, like chemical treatments, cannot be avoided and provide a steady stream of revenue. New construction was only 14% of the business last year, with renovation and remodel work another 22%. Essentially, 36% of the business is highly cyclical, while 64% is fairly steady. It is this cyclical piece that has dragged down results.

Even with this diversity, results continue to be muted. In the company’s third quarter, reported on October 23rd, Pool Corp. earned $3.39, which beat estimates by $0.02 as revenue grew 1% to $1.5 billion. Gross margins expanded 50 bps in Q3 to 29.6%. As a result, gross profit grew about 3% to $429 million. Offsetting this, operating expenses as a share of sales rose by 50 bps to 17.3%. Accordingly, operating margins were stable at 12.3%. In its key states, sales in California and Arizona were down 3%, while Texas was flat, showing some stability after a period of meaningful underperformance.

As you can see below, POOL has been raising prices of non-commodity items in order to recoup tariff costs, providing about a 2% tailwind to revenue. However, this was partially offset by lower volumes. Maintenance work was actually up slightly from a year ago, but discretionary activity was down 2% as consumers are reluctant to make a major purchase like a pool, especially in this elevated interest rate environment.

Notably, building materials spending rose for the first time in three years, admittedly off of a low base. Still, we may be nearing stabilization here. Equipment sales are also positive. While some maintenance work can be deferred for a time, it cannot be deferred forever. After several years of weak activity, there may be more quasi-discretionary maintenance that no longer can be delayed, helping to provide some support for the business.

On the last earnings call, management said that consumer sentiment is more optimistic than it was, though it expects more rate cuts will be necessary to boost demand. This is especially true among “entry-level” buyers. In general, we have seen higher-income consumption hold up better than at the low-end, a phenomenon increasingly referred to as a “K-shaped” economy. While the Fed is cutting rates, I believe we need to see long-term rates push below 6% to meaningfully alter construction-related activity.

Turning to its balance sheet, POOL is carrying $128 million of cash and $1.1 billion of debt. As such, debt/EBITDA leverage stands at 1.6x. This is a reasonable level, given the mix of the business, though it is up from 1.41x a year ago. Inventory is up 4% from last year to $1.2 billion, partially reflecting the higher cost of goods due to tariffs.

So far this year, the company has generated $237 million of free cash flow, but this included ~$134 million of working capital headwinds. Otherwise, free cash flow would be closer to $370 million. I expect some of this working capital pressure to reverse in Q4, and the company should end the year with ~$350-$375 million of free cash flow, or $9-10/share. The company has repurchased $164 million of stock so far this year. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 2.3% from last year. Shares also offer a 2.1% dividend yield.

Alongside results, the company reiterated guidance. I would note that POOL’s outlook includes $0.11 of one-time tax benefits. Back in May, I saw the company earning $11.00-$11.30. Results are not materially different than my outlook but are slightly lower at the midpoint. I expect POOL to earn ~$11 this year. My outlook drove my $225 target as I struggled to value POOL at more than 20x earnings, given my view that a sharp rebound in demand was unlikely.

To me, it does appear that Pool’s results are near, or at, their trough. After a multiyear contraction, activity is sufficiently low that it is simply unlikely to get much worse, absent a recession. While there has been some softening in the labor market, I do expect the US to avoid recession. As such, I do not expect Pool’s earnings to really fall below the ~$11 level, and we should see free cash flow improve in 2025 due to a deferred tax payment this year.

That said, I do not see a recipe for a V-shaped recovery off of this level. Labor markets are tepid, and discretionary income growth is muted. I expected housing starts and existing home sales to continue to slow, a headwind for new construction activity, especially in the Sun Belt (the Florida housing market seems particularly weak). That said, POOL will benefit from deferred maintenance activity. I also expect it to continue gaining market share, as years of weak activity have likely pushed out some marginal players.

As such, I am looking for the company to earn $10.75-$11.75 next year, depending on construction activity. I believe much of the decline in shares this year has not been because 2025 was surprisingly weak but because investors have been ratcheting down expectations for the pace of recovery, causing POOL’s multiple to compress from elevated levels. Even today, shares are trading at ~21x my 2026 expectation, which is not cheap unless one is expecting a sharp recovery beyond 2026.

On this drop, POOL is definitely not a buy in my view. A meaningful recovery is still being priced in. I would want to see shares closer to 18x before I would actually be a buyer at $205-$210. That said, I am cautious about letting a winning trade run too long. Since I first rated POOL a sell in 2023, shares have underperformed the market by ~85 percentage points, after all. There has been tremendous underperformance as markets have gradually come toward my view that construction would be slow to recover.

I view ~20x earnings as a reasonable price target, reflecting the fact POOL has consistently enjoyed a premium multiple thanks to its dominant market position and solid balance sheet. This suggests a fair value of $230, about 6% below current levels. This leaves shares a close call between a sell and a hold in my view, as no fair value estimate is exact. There is not enough downside to merit being short.

On balance, I remain a “sell,” though it is less clear-cut than in May. I come to this conclusion in part because I see modest downside. More so though, I struggle to see a case for material upside from here. A sharp rebound in construction appears unlikely, with stabilization at muted levels more likely. As such, I see shares, at best, treading water around current levels, and investors should allocate their capital elsewhere to stocks that have a clearer opportunity for upside performance.

data.title: Waterdrop: Secular Growth And A Vast Pool Of Prospects
data.description:
Thesis

I'm rating Waterdrop Inc. (WDH) as Buy, expecting a 30% upside for the next 12 months, based on a residual income model backed by the following aspects.

First, the secular growth of the industry; second, the buybacks and dividends, which are combining for around 10% per year. Third, the vast user base on the crowdfunding platform, which represents one-third of the total population in China. This user base could be a great source for future and sustainable growth.

Lastly, regulation, competition, and the increased customer bargaining power are factors that require careful consideration.

Company Overview

Waterdrop Inc. is an insurtech (insurance technology) company in China that focuses on two key activities: an insurance marketplace and a crowdfunding platform. Insurance represents 88% of total revenues, and it's the only segment showing operating profit.

This last quarter was outstanding, both QoQ and YoY. Revenues are growing 24% Y/Y on a QoQ basis in RMB and 10.97% on a TTM Y/Y basis in USD. It's also interesting to note that not only revenues but also earnings grew 36% QoQ.

Regarding the crowdfunding segment, when we analyze it from a Business Canvas model perspective, it is a key activity because it successfully acquires prospects who are subsequently offered insurance policies. Although the crowdfunding segment accounts for just 8% of total revenues and has an operating loss, it contributes indirectly yet critically to the insurance sector. The total number of users who have donated using the platform is quite high, according to Corporate Secretary Jasmine Lee in the last earnings call.

As of the end of June 2025, around 480 million people cumulatively donated CNY 70 billion to about 3.54 million patients through our Waterdrop Medical Crowdfunding platform.

To put that into perspective, China has a population of around 1,414 million people, so this means that 33.94% (480/1414) of people in China have used the platform to donate.

The company is focusing strongly on developing and improving its AI models. They use these models to scan campaigns and comments to detect and prevent potential fraud. A campaign is the "profile and story" of a person with an illness who needs a donation to fund the treatment. Now their LLM detects 15 high-risk scenarios of potential fraud. This is crucial to building and maintaining a trustworthy image of the platform and the company's reputation. If there are a lot of fraudulent campaigns, it undermines the business model.

Regarding the Chinese insurtech industry, Grandview Research is very optimistic, expecting a 54.7% CAGR until 2030. Mordor Intelligence, on the other hand, forecasts a 13.81% CAGR for the broader insurance industry. Separately, Precedence Research expects a 37.1% CAGR by 2034 for the insurtech industry. It is clearly key to distinguish between traditional insurance and insurtech projections here. While Waterdrop is growing at 24% Y/Y, it didn't see that growth until the most recent quarter. It will be critical to follow up in the next quarters to determine if this monetization of the crowdfunding platform user base is sustainable, and to what extent.

They plan to repurchase shares worth up to $50 million through September 2026. This currently represents 8.3% of the market cap, which is significant. Additionally, the board of directors approved a cash dividend of US$10.9 million (1.8% of market cap and a dividend yield of 3.03%), which was paid on November 7.

As of today, November 23, Seeking Alpha's quant rating is a Strong Buy, with an index of 4.52. Growth metrics are above the industry median, and regarding valuation, it is more modest, but depending on the ratio that we'd choose, it would indicate some degree of undervaluation. Current profitability is subpar, but it could improve in the coming years due to growth in sales and, more importantly, in earnings. Lastly, earnings revisions show a clear upward trend since May 2025. In other words, Waterdrop is accelerating growth, especially in earnings.

The company is set to release earnings on December 3 (pre-market). Analysts are projecting $0.04 for non-GAAP quarterly earnings per share; in the last quarter, it was $0.03. For the topline, they expect $120mm in revenues for the quarter—a 2.56% bump from the last one (10.6% annualized).

Now, if we forecast using the last 11 months, there is a two-sided issue. The predicted value using the native Excel function to forecast for next quarter's revenue is $106 million. This is because the last quarter was an exceptional one, so statistical models may assume some "reversion to the mean". But on the other hand, the overall trend is upward, so a figure closer to the analyst's is also reasonable. While I'm not expecting sales to decline next quarter, it's worth mentioning that some statistical models point towards a mild dip ahead.

The main reasons that I believe this acceleration of the last two quarters may be sustainable, at least to some extent, are the following. First, there was a skyrocketing increase of 80% Y/Y of first-year premiums; this implies that most of that may be new clients. Second, AI implementation is helping match and convert 50% better than traditional advertisement slots. Third, since its rollout in March, the AI service customer agent has scaled rapidly, which boosts efficiency and prevents manual bottlenecks. Fourth, there was an expansion of the product portfolio. It's worth mentioning, for example, that a new product for people with pre-existing conditions contributed CNY 300 million, making up 9.36% of the total volume (CNY 300M/CNY 3,204M).

Wrapping up, this product portfolio expansion, combined with different AI implementations, supports this new trend of the last two quarters. Because of these new rollouts, I expect revenues to be at $120mm to $125mm for the 3Q 25.

Valuation

On a relative basis, Seeking Alpha's quant rating is "B," and when these ratios are compared to the median sector, they suggest a potential upside of 78% on average at first glance. Digging deeper, we see three aspects to consider. First, the most important ratio may be the P/E Non-GAAP forward; I will discuss it later in this section. Second, sales-based ratios suggest the most upside, especially EV-based ones. As a rule of thumb, enterprise value (EV) is more robust than market cap, as it considers cash and debt. Also, stockholders need earnings (or free cash flows) instead of just sales, because they take into account expenses. While sales can be useful for comparing companies in the early stages, in most cases, earnings-based ratios are more representative for valuation. That said, even after growing 24% Y/Y (which is not a bad figure), sales-based ratios are not expensive, but quite the opposite. In other words, other companies that are growing fast are trading at double or triple the sector median; this obviously is not the case. The third aspect to consider is to factor in growth. When a company is growing fast, forward ratios are going to differ from TTM-based ones, and the greater the growth and the farther out the years ahead, the bigger the difference.

Now, from a historical perspective, the price-to-book ratio indicates a fair value zone, and the EV/revenues suggests even overvaluation, as it is trading above the historical mean. This is why I consider it critical to use different valuation models and to avoid having a narrow perspective.

Analysts forecast earnings growing around 24% Y/Y, and when we consider, for example, the 2027 ratio, it suggests a significant upside ahead. Forward price ratios are as follows:

    • FY 2025: 10.33x

    • FY 2026: 8.46x

    • FY 2027: 6.25x

When it comes to absolute valuation, here is the scenario I am considering for Waterdrop. Earnings are growing at a 13.1% CAGR in the next 10 years. The discount rate is 11.15%, which is something on the high end of American companies, to take into account the increased risk for being a Chinese company.

While these assumptions might sound optimistic, I believe they are quite conservative. The company's earnings are growing around 70% Y/Y in both TTM and quarterly. Also, the payout rate has been 10.1%, due to the combination of dividends at 1.8% plus 8.3% of share buybacks. If the company maintains this policy, and it gets factored into the model, the potential upside would be 43% for the next 12 months, according to this model. Also, it's reasonable to expect an earnings growth rate above 13%; if the trend of the last quarter is sustained, the growth rate could be higher. However, I decided to remain conservative on both aspects, given the uncertainty and risks covered in the following section.

Risks

There are three key risks for Waterdrop: regulations, competition, and high dependency on brokerage.

The biggest risk for Waterdrop is the current and future potential regulations. The insurance industry is controlled by the China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC). There are three aspects to consider here. First, the CBIRC regulates digital distribution channels, which means it addresses data privacy concerns, misleading advertising cases, and the suitability of the product itself for the client. Second, brokerage commissions. In short, the CBIRC can limit commissions to lower consumer costs. Third, it regulates the insurance policy; a common problem in China was the confusion concerning the short-term healthcare insurance. People paid for an insurance policy with a one-year coverage term due to the lower price, but the single-year limitation was not adequately communicated to them by the sellers. These kinds of issues fueled the increasing regulatory waves by the Chinese Government.

Second, competition is fierce. The are two types of competitors for Waterdrop: traditional insurers and the insurtechs. Regarding the traditional insurers, I don't believe they are a big issue, but the insurtechs are a greater threat that could squeeze the growth and shrink the margins of Waterdrop. There are three key competitors to highlight. First, Ant Group of Alibaba (BABA); their main competitive advantage is that they have Alipay, which is the biggest payment app in China. Second, there is Tencent's WeChat Pay (OTCPK:TCEHY), which has the second biggest payment app. We should keep in mind that WeChat is the top instant messaging app in China (similar to Facebook Messenger, Apple Messages, or WhatsApp), but it is far more than just messages; it's a whole ecosystem, and its user base is huge. In other words, WeChat users are just a few clicks away from getting insurance. Lastly, there is the "Ping a good Doctor" company; this one is the health division of the largest Chinese insurance company called "Ping An Insurance". The two key factors of this last competitor are its large size and its high degree of vertical integration with hospitals and doctors.

In third place, most revenue comes from commissions paid by third-party insurance partners. Waterdrop sells insurance, but doesn't underwrite the policies, which poses two threats. First, insurance companies could suddenly change the fees/rates, affecting the revenues and margins of Waterdrop. Second, they could move to "cut out the middleman" by selling directly to the customers.

Additionally, setting aside the fundamentals, we also need to consider the liquidity and volume factor. This ADR has relatively low volume. Its 20-day average is 210,000 shares, which equates to a daily value of $569K in USD. As a rule of thumb, it is not a good idea to trade over 5% to 10% of this daily mean. Please be aware of this increased liquidity risk.

Conclusion

At first glance, it initially seemed like a Hold, mainly due to trading at historical average price/book levels, combined with regulatory hurdles and fierce competitors, which create uncertainty regarding future growth and margins. However, despite these headwinds, the company had an impressive last quarter, and if this trend continues, my assumptions (earnings growing at 13% CAGR) will fall short.

There is uncertainty related to factors such as stronger competition, further regulations, or a continued macroeconomic decline in China. That said, I stated what I believe is the most likely scenario in terms of projections. There are four drivers for my assumptions: secular industry growth, the acceleration in sales and earnings growth in the last quarter, the buybacks and dividends policy, and a vast pool of potential prospects, which represent one-third of the Chinese population. All of this changed my initial impression and brought me into the bull camp.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Northern Multi-Manager Global Listed Infrastructure Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
In the third quarter of 2025, the global listed infrastructure sector, as measured by the S&P Global Infrastructure Net Index, returned 3.50%. While utilities and airports were the top performing sectors, highways and rail tracks and independent power producers (IPPs) lagged during the quarter. From a regional perspective, Canada, Western Europe and the United States outperformed while the United Kingdom and Australia lagged.

The Fund underperformed its benchmark, the S&P Global Infrastructure Net Index, during the third quarter with a return of 2.60%. The primary drivers of the underperformance was an overweight to the United Kingdom, an underweight to and stock selection in Canada, and non-benchmark positioning in communications towers. Contributors to performance included stock selection in the United States, including non-benchmark exposures to U.S. railroads, and stock selection in highways and rail tracks. Sub-adviser Lazard (LAZ) underperformed due to an overweight in the United Kingdom and an underweight to Canada. Sub-adviser First Sentier Investors outperformed on positive stock selection in utilities and out of benchmark holdings in U.S. railroads. Sub-adviser Cohen & Steers (CNS) outperformed due to positive stock selection in utilities and oil and gas infrastructure. Sub-adviser KBI Global Investors underperformed as renewable utilities lagged. Sub-adviser Eagle Global Advisors underperformed on poor stock selection in oil and gas infrastructure.

Concentration Risk: Investing a high percentage of net assets in securities in a specific industry, the Fund may be subject to greater volatility than a fund that is more broadly diversified.

Currency Risk: Foreign currencies will fluctuate in value relative to the U.S. dollar; therefore you may lose money if the local currency of a foreign market depreciates against the U.S. dollar.

Emerging and Frontier Markets Risk: Emerging and frontier market investing may be subject to additional economic, political, liquidity and currency risks not associated with more developed countries. Additionally, frontier countries generally have smaller economies or less developed capital markets than traditional emerging markets and, as a result, the risks of investing in emerging market countries are magnified in frontier countries.

Equity Risk: Equity securities (stocks) are more volatile and carry more risk than other forms of investments, including investments in high-grade fixed-income securities. The net asset value per share of this Fund will fluctuate as the value of the securities in the portfolio changes.

Foreign (Non-U.S.) Securities Risk: Investing in foreign (non-U.S.) securities may result in the Fund experiencing more rapid and extreme changes in value than a fund that invests exclusively in securities of U.S. companies, due to less liquid markets, foreign government intervention and adverse economic, political, diplomatic, financial and regulatory factors.

Infrastructure Companies Risk: Infrastructure companies may not realize projected revenue volumes due to; cost overruns; changes in terms making a project no longer economical; macroeconomic factors may raise the average cost of funding; government regulation; government budgetary constraints; special tariffs and/or changes in tax law and unfavorable accounting standards.

Liquidity Risk: Some securities held by the Fund may be difficult to sell, or illiquid, particularly during times of market turmoil. Illiquid securities also may be difficult to value. If the Fund is forced to sell an illiquid asset to meet redemption requests or other cash needs, the Fund may be forced to sell at a loss.

Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs) Risk: Investing in MLPs involves certain risks related to investing in the underlying assets of the MLPs and risks associated with pooled investment vehicles. MLPs that concentrate in a particular industry or a particular geographic region are subject to risks associated with such industry or region. The benefit derived from the Fund's investment in MLPs is largely dependent on the MLPs being treated as partnerships for federal income tax purposes, treatment of an MLP (owned by the Fund) as a corporation would materially reduce the after-tax return to the Fund with respect to its investment in the MLP. The Fund must include its allocable share of the MLP's taxable income in its taxable income, whether or not it receives a distribution of cash from the MLP. In such cases, the Fund may have to liquidate securities to make required distributions to the Fund's shareholders.

Mid-Cap Risk: Mid-capitalization stocks typically carry additional risk, since smaller companies generally have higher risk of failure and, historically, their stocks have experienced a greater degree of volatility.

Multi-Manager Risk: is the risk that the sub-advisers' investment styles will not always be complementary, which could affect the performance of the Fund.

Non-Diversified Risk: The Fund invests in a smaller number of securities than the average mutual fund. The change in value of a single holding may have a more pronounced effect on the Fund's net asset value and performance than for other funds.

Small-Cap Risk: Small-capitalization funds typically carry additional risks since smaller companies generally have a higher risk of failure. Their stocks are subject to a greater degree of volatility, trade in lower volume and may be less liquid.
